coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as Co-active materials for highly efficient dye sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Shejale, Kiran P.
Jaiswal, Arun
Kumar, Aditya
Saxena, Sumit
Shukla, Shobha
coredata.dc:description: High-quality nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are synthesized using domestic microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. These show excellent physiochemical and optical properties such as wide spectral adsorption, high charge carrier extraction, fast charge carrier transportation, and tuneable emission. These NCQDs are introduced in the dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC)/quantum dot sensitized solar cell (QDSSC) structure to improve the performance. The effect of synthesized NCQD as sensitizer, co-sensitizer, and co-photoactive layers is investigated for the DSSC/QDSSC structure. High photoconversion efficiency of 8.75% and photocurrent density of 18.13 mA/cm2 is achieved under one sun irradiation when NCQDs are used as co-photoactive layer. The obtained power conversion efficiency is approximately 55% and 99% better than NCQDs as co-sensitizer and sensitizer, respectively. The incorporation of the NCQDs in the photoactive layer synergically enhanced photo absorbance and reduced recombination between photoanode and electrolyte. A large number of anchoring sites for dye, highly conducting photoanode, fast charge carrier transportation, and inherent light-emitting photo-fluorescent property of NCQDs in mesoporous titania are understood to be responsible for this enhancement. The optimized weight ratio of citric acid and urea in the synthesis of NCQDs has provided the widened light response, low recombination rate, and high charge transport in the DSSC structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
DSSC
Microwave synthesis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, Technology Mission Division

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge financial support from the Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, Technology Mission Division through grant no. DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/100 (C) and Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) grant no. EMR/2017/005144 for this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comprehensive summary of CQD based DSSC studies. The inner circle encompasses role of CQD in a solar cell as an electrolyte, counter electrode, blocking layer at both active and counter electrodes, sensitizer, co-sensitizer with Ru-complex dyes (N719, N3 and more), co-active in photoanode with TiO2, ZnO etc and active photoanode. The middle circle summarizes techniques used for CQD synthesis such as, solid state, dialysis, acid oxidation, hydrothermal, chemical vapour deposition (CVD), microwave and electrochemical. The outer circle illustrates photoconversion efficiencies of the DSSC with respective carbon precursor as shown at left and right sides outside the circle. All further details are tabulated in the supporting information with respective references. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic of NCQD synthetic process, where citric acid and urea were used as precursors for C and N. 490 W of microwave power is used for various reaction times (1, 1.5 and 2 min). (b) FTIR spectrum of NCQD shows presence of various functional groups containing C and N, (C) TEM micrograph of NCQD with inset showing SAED pattern (d) HRTEM images of NCQD showing various interplanar distance. (e) XPS survey spectrum of NCQDs. The inset shows a bar graph representing atomic conc. [%] for N, C and O. High-resolution XPS spectra for (f) C1s, (g) O1s and (h) N1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectrum showing different transitions (b) absorbance of QDs for different reaction time (c) Fluorescence spectra of NCQD at different excitation wavelength with respecting Gaussian fit at 350 and 325 nm, (d) Time resolved photoluminescence of NCQDs with 360 nm excitation at 450 and 500 nm emission wavelength respectively and (e) shorter and longer life time at different emission wavelengths (t1, 450 & t2, 500 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a)J-V curve of NCQD DSSCs in two different combinations co-active (TiO2 (P25) +NCQD) and co-sensitizer (N718+NCQD) with inset illustrating respective DSSC device schematics, (b) EIS of co-active type NCQD DSSC with inset representing equivalent circuit and table summarizing performance of the solar cells (where FF represents the fill factor and η represents the photo conversion efficiency), (c) Schematic of energy level, charge (e−) transport and NCQD role as co-active in photoanode [bandgap for NCQD and N719 should larger and (d) Bar graph showing comparative study of NCQD devices performance with NCQD as co-active material in photoanode, with NCQDs synthesized using hydrothermal, electrochemical acid oxidation methods, where current study demonstrate NCQD by microwave method. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as Co-active materials for highly efficient dye sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanostructures Engineering and Modeling Laboratory, Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai, MH, 400076, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanostructures Engineering and Modeling Laboratory, Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai, MH, 400076, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-quality nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are synthesized using domestic microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. These show excellent physiochemical and optical properties such as wide spectral adsorption, high charge carrier extraction, fast charge carrier transportation, and tuneable emission. These NCQDs are introduced in the dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC)/quantum dot sensitized solar cell (QDSSC) structure to improve the performance. The effect of synthesized NCQD as sensitizer, co-sensitizer, and co-photoactive layers is investigated for the DSSC/QDSSC structure. High photoconversion efficiency of 8.75% and photocurrent density of 18.13 mA/cm2 is achieved under one sun irradiation when NCQDs are used as co-photoactive layer. The obtained power conversion efficiency is approximately 55% and 99% better than NCQDs as co-sensitizer and sensitizer, respectively. The incorporation of the NCQDs in the photoactive layer synergically enhanced photo absorbance and reduced recombination between photoanode and electrolyte. A large number of anchoring sites for dye, highly conducting photoanode, fast charge carrier transportation, and inherent light-emitting photo-fluorescent property of NCQDs in mesoporous titania are understood to be responsible for this enhancement. The optimized weight ratio of citric acid and urea in the synthesis of NCQDs has provided the widened light response, low recombination rate, and high charge transport in the DSSC structure.

ce:section.ce:para: Development of energy harvesting devices has gained significant attention with advancements in nanoscience and nanotechnology to fulfill the gap in current and future energy requirements due to the depletion of limited available fossil fuel [1]. Third-generation solar cells, specifically dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), have attracted a lot of attention and have emerged as promising candidates for clean renewable energy due to less toxic nature, low production cost, flexibility, and relatively high photoconversion efficiency [2,3]. However, high recombination rate and limited spectral response provide ample opportunities for further development of this technology [4]. Briefly, a typical DSSC consists of a mesoporous wide bandgap semiconductor (mostly anatase TiO2 and other metal oxides) as a photoanode and anchored with an optimized thick layer of photosensitizer molecules (Ru-complex and natural dyes), electrolyte (iodine-based), and high work function counter electrode (Pt and Au) [5]. The overall photoconversion efficiency is directly associated with photosensitizer anchoring on the photoanode, electron transport at various junctions, and photon absorption at a broad spectrum of visible range [6]. Serval studies have been reported to cover these aspects and have enabled in achieving high photoconversion efficiency. Despite high efficiency, solar cell's stability and recombination process hinder its further commercial utilization.

On the other hand, quantum dots (QD) based DSSC (known as QDSC) has exhibited promising performance to overcome these issues to achieve stability and high photoconversion efficiency [7]. Conventional DSSC structure is modified to that of QDSC's in different architectures by replacing and developing electrolytes [8], counter electrodes [9], blocking layer at both active and counter electrodes [10], sensitizer [11], co-sensitizer [12], co-active [13] and active [14] layer in photoanode. Incorporating CQDs in photoanode provides a tunable band structure along with harvesting multiple generated excitons and enhances light absorption and charge transport in the structure. Generally, the ultraviolet light exposure pronouncedly degrades the photosensitizer in conventional DSSC using dyes as a photosensitizer over a period of time and severely affects the solar cell stability, forming a bottleneck [15]. QD based DSSCs have more stability as they harvest efficiently UV light from the solar spectrum and protect the photosensitizer from degradation [16]. However, the photoconversion efficiency of these solar cells is limited by the poor linkage of QD with metal oxide, inadequate distribution of QDs over the semiconductor oxide, and narrow absorption window [17]. These shortcoming results in high recombination rate, low charge separation, and transport, resulting in low overall photoconversion efficiency. Although inorganic QDs like GaSb, PbS, CdTe, and others have reported remarkable photoconversion efficiency, their hazardous nature limits their application [18]. Moreover, carbon quantum dots offer low toxicity, large surface area, simple preparation, biocompatibility, stability, large two photon absorption, and photo-induced excellent electron transport [19]. Furthermore, in a recent report, CQD was doped with nitrogen, which resulted in the generation of a large number of active sites, tunable bandgap, and high charge transfer rate [20].

A comprehensive summary of CQD based DSSC with its role, preparation method, photoconversion efficiency, and the carbon source is shown in Fig. 1 and tabulated as Table S1 in the supplementary information. Some of the chemical and physical techniques which have been used for the synthesis of CQDs include wet oxidation, laser ablation, hydrothermal synthesis, electrochemical method, acidic oxidation, oxygen plasma etching, pyrolysis, and catalyzed cage-opening (Fig. 1 and Table S1, supporting information). Typically, these methods involve surface passivation, harsh synthetic conditions, complex process, and costly reagents, which hinder the exploration of CQDs in practical applications. Among these techniques, microwave-assisted synthesis has become more popular due to its simple one-step process, less time-consuming, and low-cost in nature. Microwave-assisted synthesis significantly reduces the dependence on surface passivation reagents [=23].

In an earlier report, in a CQD based DSSC, the CQD was used as co-sensitizer with N719, and PEG-m-CQDs resulted in 8.12% efficiency and high hole extraction [12]. In a similar study, CQD obtained from strawberry and N719 cumulatively used as a photosensitizer enabled achieving 8.09% efficiency [21]. In another study, bi-tandem CQD tailored counter electrode was fabricated, resulting in 7.87% efficiency [9]. In yet another report, efficient utilization of UV light resulting in 8.2% photoconversion efficiency was obtained due to the thin blocking layer coating of N-CQD [22]. In this study, we report the controlled synthesis of N doped CQDs by optimizing a wide range of ratio variation between citric acid and urea. One-step, environmentally friendly, and low-cost microwave synthesis are the highlights of this synthesis protocol. The prepared samples exhibited excellent optical response, and a detailed study was performed to understand photoluminescence properties. The controlled doping of nitrogen in CQD results in enhanced conductivity as well as optical absorbance due to a slightly reduced bandgap [26]. Further, these NCQDs were synergistically explored in DSSC as a sensitizer, co-sensitizer, and co-active material. As a sensitizer, the fabricated cell demonstrated low photoconversion efficiency due to less affinity towards TiO2, while devices prepared using NCQD as co-sensitizer with N719 showed enhanced performance due to widening light absorption range but were limited by aggregation and recombination effect. However, when NCQDs were used as active materials along with TiO2 have significant potential to show the highest photoconversion efficiency. The remarkable photoconversion efficiency improvement can be due to harvesting more incident photon in the composite layer of TiO2 and NCQDs. The composite substantially works on key points like a lowering effective optical bandgap, enhancement in the electron transport, a large number of active sites, and efficient electron-hole separation. Most importantly, the incorporation of NCQDs in TiO2 also facilitates the band structure modification of the photoanode and can lower the recombination between the electrolyte and the photoanode.

The detailed synthesis protocol of NCQDs has been reported elsewhere in our previous work [27]. Briefly, citric acid (CA) and urea are used as a carbon core precursor and source of nitrogen in the ratio of 3:1 and irradiated with 490 W of microwave radiations (2450 MHz) for various time intervals ranging from 1 to 2 min. The schematic of the synthesis protocol is shown in Fig. 2 (a). After cooling at room temperature, the resultant solution was washed by centrifugation at 18000 rpm for 20 min with ethanol and distilled water.

The detailed morphology and crystalline nature of NCQDs were analyzed by JOEL FEG-TEM 200 kV high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Optical properties were investigated using UV–Vis spectroscopy with a sampling interval of 0.5 and an accumulation time of 0.1 s using UV-2600 Shimadzu series spectrophotometer. Fluorescence studies were performed at scan speed of 200 nm/min with a slit width of data interval of 0.5 nm, using RF-6000 Spectro Fluorophotometer. X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was obtained using Empyrean PAN Alytical with a Cu target (Cu Kα, λ = 1.54184 Å, 40 kV &30 mA). The CHN elemental analysis (FLASH EA 1112 series) was also performed to investigate the variations in the amount of C, H, and N with changing ratio of citric acid and urea. All prepared solar cells were characterized under one sun irradiation (Enli. Tech. SS-F5-3 A) using a source meter (Keithley 2450). The surface functional groups and oxidized states of NCQD were analyzed using the AXIS Supra, Kratos Analytical X-ray photo spectroscopy (XPS).

FTO glass slide was cleaned in a bath sonicator using soap solution, DI, and HCl - Ethanol mixture (1:10) for 15 min each. After cleaning, it was treated with TiCl4 solution for 30 min at 70 °C and subsequently washed using ethanol. Screen printing was performed on this FTO slide for which paste was made using P25, ethylcellulose, alpha-tripenol, acetic acid, and ethanol in the % weight ratio of 2.1:1:2:0.6:0.36. The active surface area was maintained at 0.04 cm [2]. The same process was repeated multiple times in order to get the desired thickness (∼15 μm), and after each printing, the photoanode was sintered at 80 °C for about 10 min. The prepared photoanodes were then slowly annealed at 500 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the photoanode was immersed in 0.5 mM N719 dye solution for 24 h. Three configurations of DSSC were prepared, (a) NCQD as a dye (photosensitizer), (b) NCQD as co-sensitizer with N719 dye (NCQD:N719 dye: (1:1) and (c) NCQDs as co-active material as photoanode (NCQD:P25:6:4), NCQD concentration was maintained at 1 mg/ml.

In the first configuration, prepared photoanodes were immersed in NCQD. While in the second configuration, NCQD and N719 dye were mixed in the ratio 1:1 and prepared photoanode then immersed in it for 24 h. In the third configuration, NCQD was added directly to the TiO2 paste (6:4) before screen printing and immersed in the dye. FTO glasses were coated with Pt sol (Platisol-T) for counter electrode using a brush painting method and were annealed for 30 min at 450 °C. Both prepared dye/NCQD loaded photoanodes and Pt counter electrode were sandwiched together, and the electrolyte Iodolyte Z-50 was added in between the two electrodes. The silver conducting paste was applied on two sides of the cell and then left for drying at room temperature. The fabricated DSSC devices were further characterized to evaluate their performance.

The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2(b) shows the presence of various surface functional groups containing C and O present in prepared NCQDs. The absorption bands in between 3100 and 3500 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. These groups mainly contribute to the stability and hydrophilicity of NCQDs in the various solvent regime. Absorption bands ranging from 2800 to 2950 cm−1 corresponds to C–H band vibration (2834 cm−1). The above results indicate the presence of amine, hydroxyl, and carboxylic functional groups on the surface of synthesized NCQDs. Furthermore, absorption bands corresponding to the asymmetric (1577 cm−1) and symmetric (1419 cm−1) stretching vibrations of –COO are observed in Fig. 2(b). The bands at 1670, 1210, and 1056 cm−1 represent amide (CO), -C-O, and starching of C–N, respectively. The carbonization leads to the extra stretching vibrations of CO. The non-uniform circular morphology of synthesized NCQD was observed in TEM micrographs shown in Fig. 2(c and d). The size of NCQD was estimated to be in the range of 2–6 nm. HRTEM micrograph in Fig. 2(d) suggests that the synthesized samples lack clear borders and show both amorphous and crystalline nature. Lattice planes with interplanar spacing corresponding to 0.12, 0.18, and 0.26 nm can be observed. The SAED pattern also confirms the crystalline nature of the NCQD, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(c). The X-ray diffraction pattern of NCQDs (Supporting information, Figure S1) shows well defined broad peak at 18–38°, which indicates the formation of the very small nanoparticles. The prepared NCQDs showed a broad peak corresponding to 2θ = 23.7°, which shows disordered carbon atoms with (002) plane with hexagonal (hcp) lattice structure. Similar concurrence could be drawn by X-ray measurements performed on the photoanode film shown in Figure S2 of supporting information. TiO2 exhibited the presence of both anatase and rutile phases, but with the incorporation of NCQD, the crystallinity of both phases was decreased, and NCQD's prominent peak was observed. XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2(e) shows three characteristic peaks with binding energies corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s at 280 eV, 401 eV, and 533 eV, respectively. The Inset in Fig. 2(e) shows the atomic concentration (%) of carbon (64.77 ± 0.75), nitrogen (7.38 ± 0.66) and oxygen (27.85 ± 0.61) (see Table S3). Individual high-resolution spectra of these elements are shown in Fig. 2(f), (g), and 2(h), respectively. The deconvoluted C1s in Fig. 2(f) peak confirms the presence of CC/C–C (284.4 eV), C–N–O (286.6 eV), and O–CO (288.2 eV) bonds. Similarly, high-resolution O1s spectra in Fig. 2(g) suggests the presence of CO (531.5 eV) and C–OH (533.2 eV) functional groups and concur with the findings from C1s spectra. High-resolution spectra of N1s in Fig. 2(h) showed the presence of pyridinic N of CN–C (398.04 eV), pyrrolic N of C–N–C (399.78), and C3–N/N–N (401.48 eV). The prepared NCQD functional groups and internal bonds consist of carbonyl, carboxylic, amide, hydroxyl, and many more, which make them very active/functional.

The absorbance spectra of as-prepared NCQDs in the UV–visible range (250–800 nm) show pre-eminent three peaks followed by two very broad (shoulder) peaks as shown in Fig. 3 (a). Sharp absorbance peaks at 239, 340, and 439 nm are observed due to π-π∗ transition of CC bond, n-π∗transition of C (core), and CO/CN bonds, respectively [24]. Low energy shoulder peaks generate over the high concentration of carbon of high energy peaks (π - π∗, and n - π∗) adds additional surface functional states. The nitrogen content increases the degree of conjugation in the carbon (core). Time-dependent study was performed to examine the extent of dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization process during NCQD formation. As the reaction time increases from 1 to 2 min, it can be seen from the absorption spectrum shown in Fig. 3(b) these three processes tend to reach to saturation or completion of the reaction at 2 min; beyond this reaction, duration burning starts to occur due to complete evaporation of the solvent (DI water). The prepared NCQDs show only a single peak at 335 nm for n – π∗ transition originating from CO bond when the reaction is performed for 1 min. However, the samples synthesized with reaction duration of 1.5 and 2 s show two different broad peaks, first ∼339 and second at ∼405 nm. These resemble n – π∗ transition of CO bond and n – π∗ transition of CN bonds, respectively. It is observed that the incorporation of nitrogen in CQD reduces the optical bandgap, which is more appropriate for absorbing the larger range of the solar light spectrum. With the change in the solvent, either DI or ethanol, two prominent peaks were observed, which are consistent with prior time-dependent studies. Ethanol is more suited as a solvent for solar cell applications as water increases the recombination rate by interacting with dye, metal oxide, and electrolytes.

Functional groups with core carbon forms hybridized structure act as PL active sites by forming several surface states giving rise to PL as shown in Fig. 3(c). The NCQDs show excitation-dependent fluorescence due to the presence of core carbon, different surface states (hybridization between core carbon and functional groups), and molecular states due to the presence of fluorophore. Under ultraviolet light, prepared NCQDs exhibits bright green colour, and interestingly PL intensity is based on citric acid to urea ratio.

The prepared NCQDs were mixed with ethanol for the photoluminescence studies. As shown in Fig. 3(c), for a reaction duration of 2 min, the NCQDs showed emission ranging from 435 to 560 nm when excited at 325 and 350 nm, respectively. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peaks redshift. All reactants used during synthesis are void of any emission in the visible or near-UV region; this strongly suggests that the obtained emission originates entirely from the synthesized NCQDs. The observed emission peaks were broad due to the presence of diverse PL centers, which are understood to form due to the overlapping of two spectral bands of core carbon and their surface states. The strong fluorescence was obtained due to more CN, CO hybridization, and nitrogen-containing sites deficiency. The effect of the solvent on the NCQD fluorescence intensity is shown in Figure S4 in supporting information. Photoluminescence of NCQD was found to vary in different solvents. NCQD shows remarkable solubility in the polar solvent. In ethanol, the synthesized NCQDs show two strong, distinct peaks at 405 and 430 nm due to the presence of –OH sites.

The second peak starts dominating when the excitation wavelength is increased. This suggests the presence of another specific functional group or surface state corresponding to the emission observed at 430 nm. The excitation-dependent fluorescence is prominently observed when ethanol is used as a solvent, and it offers an advantage during solar cell preparation as well, as DI blocks excitons and charge transfer at photosensitizer and electrolyte level in the solar cell. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the NCQD showed maximum absorption at 360 nm (see Fig. 3(a)). Time resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL) studies were performed to understand the excited state dynamics of NCQDs and also to study the influence of Nitrogen concentration on the fluorescence lifetime. TRPL was performed on NCQDs at 360 nm excitation, and the emission is recorded at 450 and 500 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(d and e). The time resolved photoluminescence shows a bi exponential decay indicating the presence of two decaying mechanisms (longer lifetime̴ 9.6 ns and a shorter lifetime̴ 1.5 ns).

The synthesized NCQDs were used for preparing dye sensitized solar cells in three different combinations. Briefly, in the first configuration, the prepared NCQDs were used as a sensitizer instead of N719 dye, while in the second configuration, both (NCQD and N719) were used as co-sensitizer in the ratio of 1:1 (see lower inset of Fig. 4 (a)), and in the third configuration, during screen paste preparation NCQD were mixed with P25 (6:4) as shown in the upper inset of Fig. 4(a), the NCQDs were used as co-active material. The schematic of this device is shown in supporting information as Figure S3. In a traditional DSSC assembly, the photogenerated electron gets excited upon irradiation and flow from the sensitizer to the conduction band of TiO2, and further percolate through TiO2 to move to the FTO. The alignment of the bands should be optimally placed to enable the flow of electrons. The p25-TiO2 consists of anatase to rutile ratio of 4:1 which results in bandgap of 3 eV, leading to broadening of the adsorption band, thereby covering more part of the visible region of the solar spectrum. This leads to fast extraction with a lower recombination rate resulting in enhancement of the photo conversion efficiency. The carboxylic groups present on the NCQDs attach to the TiO2 surface and lead to the enchantment in the electron transport at neighbouring nanoparticles and hence increases photocurrent density [25]. The NCQDs show excitation–dependent photoluminescence behaviour in the visible region, which can be used to broaden the light response window and higher photon to electron injection ratio.

The current density – voltage (j-v) measurements were performed under one sun irradiation to evaluate the cell's photovoltaic characteristics, as shown in Fig. 4(a) and supporting information (Figure S5). The parameters are summarized in the Table shown as in the inset of Fig. 4(b). For comparison control cell was fabricated only with P25 and N719 dye, and device characteristics are shown in supporting information as Figure S5b. The control cell fabricated using NCQD with N179 dye (co-sensitizer) showed photo conversion efficiency of 3.92%. NCQDs have a higher energy level compared toTiO2 but lower than that of dye, which results in the hindrances during electron transfer at dye to TiO2 level and acts as new recombination centers, eventually leading to reduced solar cell efficiency. The DSSC prepared with NCQD as a sensitizer resulted in photoconversion efficiency of 0.07% (see Figure S5(a)). These significantly low efficiencies suggest the key role played by critic acid and urea over these combinations. The lower voltaic parameters and higher recombination rate can explain these low photo conversion efficiencies. NCQD as sensitizer fails to harvest photon from full spectra due to limited spectral response (UV and near vis), surface defects and internal recombination, all together it further fails to transport photogenerated electrons (see Fig. 3) [11].

Interestingly, NCQD incorporated TiO2 photoanode (as co-active material) demonstrated the highest efficiency compared to others amongst these three configurations. The NCQDs shows high absorbance for CO transition. With increased number of CO moieties, the PL results in blue shift along with bandgap broadening. This increases the photon harvesting over the broad range of the visible region of light and well distinct charge carriers, the secondary light source from excited NCQD with prolonged electron lifetime enhances photoconversion efficiencies to 8.75% with P25 as shown in Fig. 4(a) and c. The higher photoconversion efficiency (8.75%) and photocurrent density (18.13 mA/cm2)are reported with NCQDs as co-active material, the high number of carboxylic group sites on the NCQD surface provides better anchoring towards TiO2 and enables more number of active sites. Also, energy levels created due to modification of bandgap structure of photoanode with NCQDs facilities the faster electron transport from LUMO level of N719 to CB of TiO2 by adding intermediate. Such results emphasis on NCQD role as an important factor for not only improving electron transfer but also to reduce the recombination significantly. The parameters obtained from the EIS performance are shown in Fig. 4(b) and listed in Table S2. Rs, R1, C1 and τn are series resistance, charge transfer resistance, chemical capacitance and electron lifetime respectively. fmax is frequency peak, all these parameters correspond to numerous charge transfer processes happing in the DSSCs. The frequency peak is inversely prepositional to the electron lifetime and was observed to increase in the NCQDs DSSC compared to the control. This clearly indicates the decrease in the charge recombination in co-active combination. Moreover, it is found to have higher electron lifetime 0.251 ms, than control DSSC 0.063 ms, such significant enhancement in the electron lifetime directly indicates the reduced charge recombination/recombination rate in the fabricated device (NCQDs DSSC). The impact of the nitrogen doping is exhibited in the Figure S6, Significantly NCQDs possesses profound advantages, as N and C exhibits similar atomic radius prevent lattice mismatch and one more e-than C, N doping significantly favors the anchoring (at TiO2 photoanode/N719 dye) and high transportation rate.Excitation–dependent photoluminescence behaviour of NCQDs broaden the light harvesting in the visible region as a possible secondary light source, as shown in Fig. 4(c). Fig. 4(d) shows a comparative performance of the devices fabricated with NCQDs synthesized using microwave technique, photoconversion performance of current and most related studies reported in literature where CQDs synthesized from hydrothermal, electrochemical, and acid oxidation methods have been used as co-active material in photoanode (see Fig. 1).

The NCQDs are synthesized using a cost-effective combustion method by cooking the different weight ratios of citric acid and urea in a domestic microwave oven. The synthesized NCQDs are examined using various physicochemical characteristics and optimized for improved charge transportation and optical properties. Three different configurations of solar cells (DSSCs) arefabricated to understand and explore the potential of synthesized NCQD for DSSC application. The high photoconversion efficiency of 8.75% is achieved by using NCQDs as co-active material in DSSC structure. The ability to achieve high photoconversion efficiency by introducing NCQDs at photoanode as co-active material suggests that the NCQDs most importantly participates as secondary source of light along with incident photon due to their spectral response over excitation, where it widens the absorption spectral response and faster electron-hole separation along with high charge transport. Such behavior is also supported by a large number of sites for anchoring N719 dye and high conductivity by NCQD insertion. This significant contribution of optimized NCQDs in achieving high performance of DSSCs provides a new pathway for facile approach/combination for NCQD based DSSC and opens up a promising area of more robust nature NCQD for energy harvesting devices.

Kiran P. Shejale: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Validation, Investigation, Project administration. Arun Jaiswal: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Aditya Kumar: Investigation, Formal analysis. Sumit Saxena: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Shobha Shukla: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge financial support from the Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, Technology Mission Division through grant no. DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/100(C) and Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) grant no. EMR/2017/005144 for this study.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.06.090.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: All-weather solar cells: a rising photovoltaic revolution
Chem. Eur J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Tang, All-weather solar cells: A rising photovoltaic revolution, Chemistry - A European Journal, 2017, 23, 8118-8127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical cells
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Gratzel, Photoelectrochemical cells, Nature, 2001, 414, 338-344.

Sol. Energy
